title: Mango chow
subtitle: Trinidadian green mango salad with lime, garlic, and scotch bonnet
excerpt: Green, unripe mango sliced and tossed with lime juice, garlic, scotch bonnet, and coriander. Sharp, hot, and refreshing — the Trinidad street snack eaten from a plastic bag.
sourceNotes: Mango chow is a Trinidadian street snack and condiment made from unripe green mango — the same mango used for picking before it sweetens on the tree. The sharpness of the unripe fruit combined with lime, scotch bonnet, garlic, and shadow beni (culantro) makes a vivid, refreshing salad. It is sold in plastic bags from vendors at markets and roadside spots across Trinidad. Shadow beni (culantro, Eryngium foetidum) has a stronger, slightly citrusy flavour compared to coriander; fresh coriander is the standard substitute. Unripe mango can be found at Caribbean and South Asian grocers.

recipe.makeAheadNotes: Best eaten within 30 minutes of dressing, before the mango softens. The mango can be peeled and sliced an hour ahead; add lime and salt just before serving.

content.text: Mango chow is not a complicated dish. It is green mango, lime, salt, garlic, scotch bonnet, and shadow beni — the herb from a roadside bush in Trinidad that has no good substitute anywhere else, so coriander stands in. The mango must be unripe: firm, sharply tart, with none of the sweetness of a ripe fruit. The whole thing takes 10 minutes to make and is gone in less than that.

Unripe mango is available at most Caribbean and South Asian grocery shops. If you can only find ripe mango, the result will be sweet rather than sour — a different dish, less interesting. Underripe mango (firm but beginning to yellow at the edges) is a reasonable middle option.

items.prepNote: green, unripe — firm and tart, not ripe

content.text: Peel the {{mango}} mangoes and cut the flesh away from the stones in thick slices or wedges. You can also shred the flesh into thin strips if you prefer. Place in a bowl. Add the {{garlic}} of garlic, {{scotch-bonnet}} scotch bonnet slices, the juice of the {{lime}} lime, and {{sea-salt-fine}} of salt. Toss well. Taste — the balance should be sharp and salty, with significant heat. Tear the {{coriander}} of coriander over the top and toss once more. Eat immediately.

items.fix: Use only firm green mango. The flesh of an unripe mango is pale, starchy, and very tart — if it tastes sweet, it will not make proper chow.

items.cause: Scotch bonnet removed or seeds discarded
items.fix: Mango chow is traditionally very hot. Include the scotch bonnet seeds for full heat. Add a second half if it still tastes mild.

items.cause: Salt and lime juice breaking down the flesh
items.fix: Dress just before eating. Salted mango releases liquid quickly; the result becomes watery if left to sit.

items.cause: Garlic too finely minced; quantity too high for raw application
items.fix: Use a very small amount of crushed garlic or slice it into thin slivers rather than mincing it to a paste.

content.text: Mango chow is street snack territory in Trinidad, sold from vendors who carry the peeled mango in bags and dress each portion to order. It is eaten at the beach, at the market, and at cricket matches. The pleasure of it is entirely in the balance: the tartness of green mango, the citric punch of lime, the sharp garlic, and the scotch bonnet heat building at the back of the throat. It wakes you up in the heat and costs almost nothing to make.
